title: Raise the Paddy Procurement Price to Rs. 2,000!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. The Tamil section is a source-checked reading copy derived from the OCR and does not alter the canonical source file. Visual inspection restored the opening `உடன்பிறப்பே,`, omitted by OCR, and confirmed the minimum-support-price increase as `50 ரூபாய்` where OCR mixed Tamil and Latin-style digits. Other changes are limited to unambiguous glyph errors and broken word joins. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: I trust that you understood the truths I conveyed indirectly in yesterday’s leaflet-like letter titled “Understand the Subtle Design!” They kept writing to ask why the DMK had taken no action against certain members who acted hastily and made mistakes during the election. They described some district secretaries as petty chieftains and declared that the movement would never act against them.

The moment the movement acted, contrary to their expectation, they cried: “Alas, action against these people? What great men of sacrifice they are!” They then met the union secretaries against whom action had been taken, interviewed them and published page after page. As I wrote yesterday, their entire purpose springs from their yearning to somehow destroy our Dravidian movement. I explained it so that you would understand and act accordingly.

Following that letter, I wish today to discuss a major demand of the farmers in the delta districts of Thanjavur, Nagapattinam and Tiruvarur. The Union Cabinet has approved an increase of Rs. 50 per quintal in the minimum support price for paddy during this year’s kharif season, fixing it at Rs. 1,360. It has also approved an increase of Rs. 55 per quintal for fine-grade paddy, fixing its minimum support price at Rs. 1,400.

Since the AIADMK came to power in May 2011, Tamil Nadu’s farmers have been struck by one problem after another: repeated crop failure, severe drought, inadequate credit, irregularities in crop insurance, discrimination in drought relief, and livestock affected by foot-and-mouth disease. Like blows falling repeatedly upon an already wounded leg, their suffering has become immeasurable. Those ruling through the AIADMK treat the farmers with neglect and indifference.

Paddy is cultivated on approximately 22 lakh hectares in Tamil Nadu. Among the Cauvery delta districts, it is cultivated throughout Tiruvarur, Nagapattinam and Thanjavur, and in parts of Tiruchirappalli, Pudukkottai and Cuddalore.

Several lakhs of agricultural labourers who once worked in the delta districts have now turned to brick kilns, Tiruppur export companies, building construction and other occupations for their livelihood. Consequently, labour is unavailable for agricultural work in the delta.

Higher rents are demanded for tractors used to plough fields and for machines used in transplanting and harvesting. When asked why, they cite the rise in diesel prices. The prices of inputs such as seed paddy, fertiliser and pesticides have also risen to unprecedented levels. With cultivation expenses and agricultural input prices now so high, farmers naturally wait and yearn for a remunerative price for paddy.

When the DMK came to office for the fifth time in 2006-2007, the price was fixed at Rs. 650 a quintal for fine-grade paddy and Rs. 620 for ordinary paddy. We raised it progressively, reaching Rs. 1,100 for fine-grade paddy and Rs. 1,050 for ordinary paddy in 2009-2010.

Last year, the Tamil Nadu Civil Supplies Corporation announced a procurement price of Rs. 1,415 a quintal for fine-grade paddy and Rs. 1,360 for ordinary paddy. These figures included Tamil Nadu government subsidies of Rs. 70 and Rs. 50 respectively, added to the Union government’s minimum support prices.

Commenting at the time, Arupathi Kalyanam, general secretary of the federation of farmers’ associations in Thanjavur, Tiruvarur and Nagapattinam districts, told reporters: “The Tamil Nadu government must provide a procurement price of at least Rs. 2,000 a quintal.”
Recognising the difficulties faced by paddy and sugarcane farmers, the DMK General Council passed a resolution stating:

“In view of today’s rise in general prices, input costs and agricultural expenditure, farmers have demanded Rs. 2,000 a quintal for paddy. Accepting that demand, this government must come forward to fix and provide a remunerative procurement price of Rs. 2,000 a quintal, slightly above the cost of production.”

In an interview with reporters in Thanjavur yesterday, Tamil Nadu Farmers’ Associations state general secretaries Shanmugam and Durai Manickam said: “The Union government has announced an increase of Rs. 50 per quintal for paddy. This has disappointed farmers. Paddy must therefore be procured at Rs. 2,000 a quintal.” They also announced demonstrations in Thanjavur, Tiruvarur and Nagapattinam on 5 July.

But adopting the attitude, “Whoever says it, what does it matter? Whoever asks, what does it matter?”, the government listened neither to farmers’ association leaders nor to opposition parties. Acting unilaterally, it fixed Rs. 1,415 a quintal for fine-grade paddy and Rs. 1,360 for ordinary paddy.

Now that the Union government has raised the paddy price to Rs. 1,360 a quintal, how much more will the Tamil Nadu government add? Will it convene a tripartite meeting, obtain suggestions there, and fulfil the farmers’ wish by raising the price to Rs. 2,000 a quintal? Or will it again act unilaterally? If it raises the price, by how much?
The Tamil Nadu government must make and announce an appropriate decision on all these questions.
Udanpirappē, I trust that you will explain these facts to our great farming community and tell them that the DMK will always stand by their demand.
